summary: Reis argues that labeling products as 'AI-powered' is meaningless marketing fluff, comparing it to the absurdity of advertising a 1950s clock as a 'plastic clock.' The technology should serve the product's utility invisibly, not be the selling point itself.
key_points: AI and data should serve product functionality, not be the product's identity
Customers care about products that work, not the technology behind them
The 'AI-powered' label has become ubiquitous and meaningless marketing speak
money_quotes: I can't imagine the manufacturer advertising the clock as a 'plastic clock.' It's just a clock that happens to include some plastic.
What does a product have to do with data or AI? If a product uses data or AI, it's there to serve the functionality and utility of the product, not the other way around.
I don't believe the end customer cares whether a product is 'AI-powered.' People want products that work.
key_insight: Technology like AI should be invisible infrastructure that makes products better, not a marketing badge that substitutes for actual utility.
